classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Warren Deposit Bank
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Warren Deposit Bank by adding new commissioners and modifying the payment schedule for stock. Specifically, it adds W. S. Vanmeter, E. B. Seeley, S. W. Coombs, Wm. Brown, John V. Sprowle, and C. G. Smallhouse to the list of commissioners and changes the stock payment requirements to five dollars per share upon reaching a $25,000 subscription total, and twenty dollars per share ten days thereafter.

It details their authority to manage public works like sidewalks and streets, abate public nuisances, and grant or refuse licenses for various establishments including coffee-houses, taverns, inns, and shows. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Versailles and Lexington Railroad Company," approved March 11, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Versailles and Lexington Railroad Company. It grants the board of directors the power to reopen subscription books for capital stock, increase capital stock up to one million dollars, and borrow money up to four hundred thousand dollars, secured by mortgage. It also vests the board with powers for constructing a railroad from the Kentucky River in Woodford County to Lexington, through or near Versailles. The company is granted the right to enter land for surveying and to acquire the right-of-way, not exceeding sixty-six feet in width, with provisions for compensation for damages caused by engineers and agents.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details the incorporation and operational powers of a railroad company. It discusses financial matters, land acquisition for the railroad route, and the rights and responsibilities of the company and landowners. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Owensboro to levy taxes for common school purposes. The statute also addresses the condemnation of private property for school purposes and the forfeiture of lands to the Commonwealth for the benefit of the school board.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the Common School Districts in Clinton County.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the court of claims for Clinton County to levy a poll tax on male white citizens over twenty-one years of age to fund the construction or repair of schoolhouses in districts lacking comfortable facilities. The sheriff is responsible for collecting these taxes and distributing them to the respective district trustees. The levy can be made annually until sufficient funds are raised.

While it does not establish segregation or deny rights based on race, the explicit mention of 'white' in a context that could imply exclusion of other races, even if not the primary intent of this specific act, warrants classification as 'explicit' racial indicator. However, the act itself does not appear to be a Jim Crow law as it focuses on funding schoolhouse improvements and does not impose discriminatory practices or restrictions on non-white citizens. Therefore, 'is_jim_crow' is 'no'.

An earlier act consolidating these roads is repealed.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Louisville, Harrodsburg and Richmond Railroad Company," approved March 11, 1867, and to authorize the county courts of Barren, Metcalfe and Cumberland counties, or the trustees of any town in said counties, to subscribe to the stock of said company, and to levy and collect taxes for the payment of the same.
classification.summary: This act allows the county courts of Barren, Metcalfe, and Cumberland counties, or town trustees within those counties, to subscribe to the stock of the Louisville, Harrodsburg and Richmond Railroad Company. It authorizes the imposition of a tax, not exceeding thirty cents per hundred dollars of property, to create a sinking fund for the extinguishment of the principal of bonds issued for this subscription. A board of commissioners, composed of the county judge, county court clerk, and treasurer or special commissioner of the company, is established to manage this sinking fund. The act also details the process for issuing receipts for tax payments and converting them into company stock.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial and administrative aspects of a railroad company and the ability of local governments to levy taxes to support it. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on fiscal matters and corporate governance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

WHEREAS , The Iron Works Turnpike Road Company ,

county , attempted to organize and become an incorporated

vised Statutes,and said company has proceeded to make

county,tothe intersectionof said road with the George-
town and South Elkhorn Turnpike,in Scottcounty; and
whereas,saidroad company institutedand carriedon all
theirproceedings requiredto be done in court in the Fay-
ettecounty court,and no proceedingswere had in the
Scottcountycourt; and forthisreasonitis probablethat
saidcompany isnot lawfullyincorporated; therefore,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :

Works Turnpike Road Company ," and theirsuccessors,
be ,and they are hereby ,made a body corporate,who ,by
thatname and style,shallhave perpetualsuccession,and
may sue and be sued,and have all the rights and privi-
leges itwould have been entitledto had itpursued allthe
requirements of chapter 103 of the Revised Statutes to
make ita corporation,and itshallbe governed by the
provisionsof said chapter,so far as the same are appli-
cable .

road from itspresent terminus in Scott county ,along the
routeof the Iron Works Road ,to itsintersectionwith the
Georgetown and FrankfortTurnpike, or as far on said
routeas theydeem advisable; and forthat purpose the
capitalstockof said company may be increasedby the
sum of ten thousand dollars,in sharesof fiftydollarseach ;
and itshallhave the same powers ,under the same restric-
tions, and subject to the same duties and liabilities,it
would have been,had itbeen originallyincorporatedwith
authorityto build itsroad to the Georgetown and Frank-

classification.summary: This act authorizes the Concord and Tollsboro Turnpike Road Company to issue bonds up to one thousand dollars per mile, not exceeding ten percent interest per annum, to aid in the construction of unfinished portions of the road. It also allows the company to use authorized taxes and tolls collected from the finished portions of the road, after maintenance, to pay off the principal and interest of these bonds. The act mandates annual settlements with the county judge of Lewis County, detailing all financial transactions, with vouchers required for all expenditures. These settlements are to be recorded by the county clerk, and the judge and clerk will receive compensation for these services.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes for the payment of the bonded indebtedness of counties, cities and towns, and for other purposes," approved March 10, 1871.
classification.summary: This act details the appointment and duties of a Board of Commissioners of the Sinking Fund for counties, cities, or towns. It outlines the process for appointing commissioners, their oath of office, the appointment of a treasurer, bond requirements for the treasurer, and compensation. It also specifies that taxes levied under the act will be collected by the sheriff of the county or the designated tax collector of a city or town, who must execute a bond for faithful collection and payment. The act also grants powers to these collecting officers.

The act also allows for the appointment of a wharf-master, who will be recommended to the Boyd county court, take an oath, and execute a bond for the faithful performance of their duties. The wharf-master is responsible for collecting dues at established rates and for any damages resulting from illegal seizures or collections.

Before levying the tax or issuing bonds, the judge must submit the propositions to the qualified voters of Logan county, and the judge will act according to the majority vote.

stitutionand laws before a justiceof the peace .

next regular election.
§ 10. The board of trusteesshall have power to enact
all necessary ordinances and by -laws for the government
of said town ,and for securing of peace and good order
therein,and forthe preservationof the comfort and health
of the people thereof,provided they do not conflictwith
the Constitution and laws of this State .
§ 11. The trusteesshallkeep a recordof alltheirpro-
ceedings in a book kept forthat purpose .
§ 12. No fine of more than twenty dollars shall be as-
sessed fora violationof the ordinances or by -laws of said
town ; and shallbe recovered upon warrant in the name
of the Commonwealth for the use of the board of trustees .
§ 13. All moneys collectedfor violation of the ordi-
nances or by -laws of said town shallbe paid over tothe
board of trusteesby the marshal, and expended on the
streets of said town .
§14 . In the collectionof fines,the marshal shallhave
all the powers of a constable, and subject to the same
rulesand regulations,and subjectto the same penalties
imposedby law in similarcases.
§ 15. The police judge shall be a conservator of the
peace; and shallkeep a record-book and execution-book ,
in which he shall make all necessary entries; and the
judge of the Morgan county court shallfixthe time for
holding hiscourtsforthe trialof civilcauses as isrequired
in justices' courts.

same fees as justicesof the peace and constables are
allowed by law for similarservices.

Approved March 18, 1871.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, Widows' and Orphans' Home
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, Widows' and Orphans' Home, establishing it as a body corporate with perpetual succession. It outlines the powers of the corporation, including the ability to sue and be sued, contract, hold and dispose of property up to $500,000, and enforce subscriptions. The institution's object is to provide a home for destitute widows and orphans of deceased members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, and others under its charge, operating under the patronage of the Louisville Annual Conference. The act also details membership structure and the election of directors.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a religious organization for charitable purposes, specifically to provide a home for widows and orphans of deceased members of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the purpose and scope of the act, which is purely charitable and religious in nature, without any racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mt. Sterling
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Mt. Sterling by establishing a board of councilmen consisting of a mayor and four councilmen, elected annually. It details the division of the town into four wards, the qualifications for councilmen (resident and freeholder), and the mayoral election process. It also outlines the oath of office for officials, the mayor's duties in presiding over meetings and enforcing town laws, and grants the board corporate powers. The act vests the new board with the rights and assets of the former trustees.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mt. Sterling
classification.summary: This act grants the mayor and board of councilmen of Mt. Sterling broad powers to manage town property, make conveyances, improve streets, regulate public health (including establishing hospitals and pest-houses, and enforcing quarantine laws), declare and abate nuisances, suppress vice (gaming, drunkenness, disorderly houses), regulate the storage of combustible materials, and organize a fire company. It also grants jurisdiction for these matters to the police judge of Mt. Sterling.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the municipal powers granted to the town of Mt. Sterling in 1871. It covers a range of administrative and regulatory functions, including property management, public works, public health, and law enforcement within the town. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted are general in nature and apply to all inhabitants of the town. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mount Sterling
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Mount Sterling. It repeals the authority of the board of council to license taverns but allows existing licenses to remain valid. It clarifies that this repeal does not affect the sale of spirituous liquors by merchants. It also outlines licensing requirements for taverns, groceries, victualers, confectioneries, liquor retailers, and nine or ten-pin alleys, requiring separate licenses from the Montgomery county court clerk in addition to board of council licenses. The act also grants the board of councilmen the power to borrow money for municipal purposes, with limitations and voter approval requirements for amounts exceeding one thousand dollars. Finally, it empowers the board to appoint four policemen with the same arrest powers as sheriffs or constables, and to erect a station house and workhouse.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal governance of Mount Sterling, Kentucky, specifically concerning licensing for various businesses and the town's financial and law enforcement powers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The licensing requirements and powers granted are general in nature and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow segregationist policies. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mount Sterling
classification.summary: This act details the establishment and management of a police station-house and work-house in Mount Sterling. It outlines who can be confined, the conditions for confinement (including for violations of ordinances, vagrancy, bawdy-house keeping, and failure to make bail), and the ability for those confined to work off fines and costs at a set daily rate. It also grants the mayor and council power to compel able-bodied persons to work on public streets and alleys. Additionally, it grants the board of councilmen power to license auctioneers and tax peddlers and itinerant merchants, as well as domestic animals running at large.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the operational framework for a town's police and work-house facilities, including provisions for confinement, labor to pay fines, and the regulation of certain businesses and animals. While it deals with incarceration and labor, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The provisions apply generally to all individuals who fall under the categories described, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Mount Sterling
classification.summary: This act details various administrative and legal procedures for the town of Mount Sterling, including arrest protocols, prosecution of ordinance violations, financial management, and voter qualifications for town elections. It specifies that prosecutions for ordinance breaches shall be in the name of the Commonwealth for the benefit of the town, and fines are to be paid to the town treasurer. It also outlines voter eligibility for town elections, requiring male citizens over twenty-one, residing in the town for twelve months, or male citizens over twenty-one living outside the town but within the voting precinct who own taxable real estate within the town. Finally, it addresses the commitment of persons to jail until fines and costs are paid or replevied, with a maximum imprisonment duration tied to the fine amount.

It covers aspects like the prosecution of ordinance violations, financial management, and voter eligibility for town elections. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Flemingsburg, in the County of Fleming
classification.summary: This statute grants the board of trustees of Flemingsburg the power to suppress tippling-houses, gambling-houses, and to punish disorderly conduct, disturbing the public peace, indecent conduct or language, disturbing religious worship, schools, public speaking, lectures, or any lawful public meeting, injury to public property, and to prevent the exhibition of stud-horses or jacks on the streets, or obscene or immoral shows or pictures. It also outlines the general powers of the trustees and the appointment of a clerk.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of public order and conduct within the town of Flemingsburg. It grants the board of trustees powers to enact ordinances and impose penalties for various offenses such as operating tippling-houses, gambling-houses, rioting, disturbing the peace, and indecent conduct. It also includes provisions for preventing certain exhibitions and the display of obscene materials. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Eminence, Kentucky, establishing its boundaries, corporate powers, and municipal government. It specifies that the municipal government shall consist of a board of five trustees who must be free white citizens of the United States, residents, and owners of real estate within the town. The act also outlines the election process for these trustees and other town officers.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY, 1871
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1871 outlines the powers and responsibilities of a board of trustees in a town. It details their authority to license and tax liquor vendors, suspend licenses for unlawful gaming, disorderly conduct, or indecent behavior, and suppress various establishments and activities such as tippling-houses, bawdy-houses, riots, breaches of the peace, fornication, adultery, vagrancy, and disturbances. The trustees also have the power to regulate animals in streets, abate nuisances, and pass ordinances for the town's governance, health, and prosperity, provided they are consistent with state and federal law. Additionally, they are empowered to assess, levy, and collect taxes on real and personal estates and choses in action.
classification.reasoning: The statute grants broad regulatory powers to a town's board of trustees concerning licensing, public order, and taxation. While it addresses issues like 'bawdy-houses' and 'disorderly conduct,' there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The powers granted are general in nature and do not appear to be designed to discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

The act's effectiveness is contingent on a majority vote of the town's voters.

classification.title: An Act to Approve and Confirm an Agreement of Compromise Between Two Factions of the First Presbyterian Church of Shelbyville, and to Incorporate Said Factions
classification.summary: This act ratifies and confirms a compromise agreement between two factions of the First Presbyterian Church of Shelbyville, which had divided in September 1866. The agreement details the division of church property, debts, and records, and establishes two distinct corporate bodies. One faction, led by Dr. Matthews, is to receive the parsonage property. The other faction, led by Mr. Grasty, agrees to give possession of the parsonage by April 1, 1871. Both factions are incorporated as separate entities under the name 'First Presbyterian Church of Shelbyville'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Farmers' Turnpike Road Company," approved March 3d, 1851.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from 1851 concerning the Farmers' Turnpike Road Company. It increases the authorized capital stock to $25,000, designates specific locations (Burlington and Belleview) and commissioners for opening stock subscription books, repeals section four of the original act, and modifies section five to trigger the election of company officers upon subscription of 250 shares. It also grants the company the same toll rates as the Burlington and Florence Turnpike Road Company, increases the treasurer's bond to $10,000, and specifies the road's commencement point in Burlington. The act also limits stock calls to $5 per share at any one time.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to an act incorporating a private company for building a turnpike road. It details financial aspects, organizational procedures, and operational details of the company. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Licking and Lexington Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act concerns the financial obligations and operational rights of the Licking and Lexington Railroad Company. It outlines procedures for contesting elections related to the company's bond issuance, details how cities and counties should levy taxes to pay the company, and grants the company the right to acquire corporate rights in other Southern states. It also permits consolidation with other railroads, with a proviso to maintain service to specific cities. Notably, sections 3 and 4 are exempted for Kenton County and the city of Covington.

classification.title: An Act for the benefit of the personal representatives of Robert Foster, deceased.
classification.summary: This act allows the personal representatives of Robert Foster, deceased, to file a petition in the Franklin circuit court to establish the existence, ownership, and loss of a bond and its coupons. The court is granted jurisdiction to hear and determine the case as if it were between private persons. The Attorney General is tasked with representing the state's interests. If the court finds the bond existed, was owned by Foster, and was lost, it can enter a judgment for the bond's amount and accrued interest. The Auditor of Public Accounts is to draw a warrant for the judgment amount, provided the plaintiffs post a bond to indemnify the Commonwealth against damages from future presentation of the lost bond or coupons. Plaintiffs are also responsible for court costs.
keywords: Board of Commissioners of the Sinking Fund
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns a legal process for the estate of a deceased individual, Robert Foster, to recover a lost bond and its associated interest from the state's Sinking Fund. It outlines the procedures for filing a lawsuit, the court's jurisdiction, the role of the Attorney General and Auditor, and the requirements for indemnifying the state. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the City of Louisville.
classification.summary: This act amends section eleven of the charter of the city of Louisville, approved March 3, 1870. It designates Third Street as a dividing line for the city into eastern and western districts for purposes of drainage, bridge and culvert construction, and public parks. Expenses for these improvements in each district will be assessed on the property within that district. The establishment of any park or pleasure ground requires voter approval from the affected district and public notice.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Anderson County Court to levy a tax to build bridges, and for other purposes.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Anderson County Court to levy an ad valorem tax, in addition to the capitation tax, not to exceed twenty-five cents on every one hundred dollars of real and personal property value. The purpose of this tax is to fund the building of bridges and the repair of roads within the county. The tax levy must be made between September 1st and April 30th, and no more than one levy can be made per year. The sheriff is responsible for collecting the tax, with powers to distrain and sell property if necessary. The act also requires a vote of the qualified voters of Anderson County to approve the levying and collection of this tax before it can be implemented.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the authorization for Anderson County to levy a tax for infrastructure improvements (bridges and roads). It outlines the process for levying, collecting, and using these funds, including the requirement for a voter referendum. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act legalizes actions taken by the Pendleton County Court in 1870 and 1871, specifically borrowing money and issuing bonds to fund the construction of bridges in the county. Doubts had arisen regarding the legality of these actions, and this act serves to validate them.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the legalization of financial transactions and public works projects undertaken by a county court. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the condemnation of land for Cemetery purposes in Pendleton County.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the presiding judge of the Pendleton County court to issue a writ of ad quod damnum for the condemnation of land, not exceeding two acres, for cemetery purposes. The proceedings are to conform to existing statutes, with damages paid by the applicant. Title vests upon confirmation of the inquest. Land can only be condemned if situated within one half mile of a church or other place of religious worship, and no existing dwelling-house or building can be taken.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the condemnation of land for cemetery purposes in Pendleton County. It outlines the legal process for acquiring land for burial sites, including the role of the county judge, sheriff, and the payment of damages. The statute does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or restrictions that were characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Kentucky River Salt Manufacturing Company
classification.summary: This act grants corporate powers to the Kentucky River Salt Manufacturing Company, allowing it to contract, acquire and hold property, sue and be sued, and establish by-laws. It details the company's powers to purchase, acquire, and hold real estate and mining privileges, search for and process various minerals (iron, lead, copper, salt, oil, coal), erect mills, trade in merchandise and stock, buy and sell land, and transport its products. The company can also invest in or build railroads and construct roads and bridges. The act specifies the capital stock limits and the location of its principal office.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act of incorporation for a private company, the Kentucky River Salt Manufacturing Company. It outlines the company's business powers, property rights, and financial structure. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to authorize the city of Newport to supply itself and others with pure water, and to establish Water-works," approved January 26th, 1871.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law authorizing the city of Newport to construct and operate waterworks. It allows the city council to sell, convey, or dispose of the waterworks, or parts thereof, to individuals, trustees, or corporations. However, any sale must include provisions for the continued supply of pure water to Newport at an equitable pro-rata cost, not exceeding five cents per thousand gallons. Water used for extinguishing fires, public buildings, or other public uses is free. The sale cannot transfer control or regulation of water rates to consumers in Newport, and these rates cannot exceed those charged by the Cincinnati waterworks board. The sale price must be at least the actual cost of the waterworks, and the proceeds are to be applied to the liquidation of debt.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the sale and operation of waterworks in the city of Newport, Kentucky. It details provisions for the sale of the waterworks, the pricing of water, and the regulation of rates. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Beargrass Transportation Company, authorizing them to construct, maintain, and operate railway branches towards Middletown and Jeffersontown, Kentucky. It also empowers the company to sell or lease these branches. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Town of Brooksville, Bracken County, to prohibit hogs from running at large within the town limits. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act grants city councils the authority to regulate city taxation, allow for the performance of work by individuals confined in city correctional facilities outside of those facilities (but within city limits), and to ordain regulations for their restraint and security. It also empowers city officers to arrest felons, gamblers, rioters, vagrants, and persons of disorderly conduct, and to summon witnesses. Additionally, it allows for increased taxes for street repair and for the procurement or construction of city correctional facilities and poorhouses.

It modifies provisions regarding the number of pupils admitted (to not exceed accommodations) and clarifies how property is to be deeded to the association. It also details the composition and election of the board of trustees.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to an act incorporating a German School Association. It deals with administrative and operational aspects of the school, such as pupil capacity and trustee elections. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the Jailer of Jefferson County.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the jailer of Jefferson County to employ four guards and sets their monthly compensation at $84, with a provision that any amount exceeding $2 per day will be paid from the county levy.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the employment and compensation of guards for the Jefferson County jail. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Town of Nebo, in Hopkins county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Nebo in Hopkins County, Kentucky. It defines the town's boundaries and establishes a system of governance with four trustees and a police judge, who also serves as chairman of the board. The act specifies the election process for these officials, their term length, and eligibility requirements, including residency and age.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation of a town and its governance structure. It outlines the election of officials and their qualifications. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Town of Nebo
classification.summary: This act establishes the Board of Trustees for the Town of Nebo, granting them corporate status and outlining their powers. These powers include managing streets, alleys, and sidewalks; enacting by-laws for town governance; levying taxes on male persons over 21 and on property; taxing auction sales; and declaring and abating nuisances. The trustees are also responsible for appointing town officers and ensuring they fulfill their duties.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and governance of the Town of Nebo, outlining the powers and duties of its Board of Trustees. It covers aspects like corporate status, management of public spaces, taxation, and the appointment of officers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Vanceburg
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and powers of town trustees, treasurer, and collector in the town of Vanceburg, Kentucky. It details procedures for collecting taxes, issuing licenses for shows and exhibitions, and the process for levying upon and selling delinquent property. It also specifies redemption periods for real property sold for taxes, with longer periods for non-residents and infants, femes covert, idiots, and lunatics.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and financial operations of a town government, specifically concerning tax collection and property sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The redemption periods for property are based on residency status (non-resident) and legal status (infants, femes covert, idiots, lunatics), not race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Ashbottom Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates 'The Ashbottom Turnpike or Gravel Road Company' for the construction of a turnpike or gravel road in Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines the company's corporate status, capital stock, and appoints commissioners to receive subscriptions. The act also reserves the right for the legislature to repeal, alter, or amend it and states it takes effect upon passage.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a turnpike road company. It details the company's formation, capital, and management. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

dom accordingto law ; and whereas, itfurtherappears
that the said Peregrine Pickle owned a tractof land con-
veyed tohim by Benjamin Shackleford,guardianof Geo .

of Kentucky ,in lotNo. 11, north of Green river,in Hen-
derson & Co's. grant,contains two hundred acres,and is
bounded as follows,viz: Beginning at the mouth of Grif-
fith'screek, into Green river; thence running north 29
degrees,east 400 poles,to a hickory and two ash trees in
an oldline; thencewith saidoldlinewestwardly 81 poles

and thence up the said river,with itsmeanders,to the
beginning; and whereas , the said Peregrine Pickle has
departedthislife,having attemptedtodisposeof thesaid
land by his lastwilland testament,and that said willdid
not take effectby reason that the beneficiariestherein
were slaves,and the said will was never executed ; and
it being representedthat the beneficiariesthereinhave
departedthislife,except a son of said Peregrine,named
Moore ,and that the said Moore isa man of good charac-
ter,who has faithfullydischargedthe dutiesof his posi-
tion; therefore,Be itenactedby the General Assemblyof the Commonwealth

Pickleall the right,title,claim,and interestof the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky in and to the said tractof land
above described, under and by virtue of the laws of
escheat,and all the right, title,and interestof the said

cheats .

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Moore Pickle (colored).
classification.summary: This act grants Moore Pickle, a Black man, all rights, title, claim, and interest of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in a specific tract of land. This land was previously owned by his father, Peregrine Pickle, who had been emancipated. Peregrine's will was invalid because the beneficiaries were slaves, and the land would have escheated to the Commonwealth. The act rectifies this by transferring the land to Moore Pickle.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored' and 'negro man slave' in its preamble and title, indicating a racial dimension. However, the act itself is not a Jim Crow law. Instead, it appears to be a corrective measure to ensure that property inherited by a Black individual, whose father's will was invalidated due to the beneficiaries being slaves, is transferred to him. This is an act for the benefit of a Black individual, not a law that restricts rights based on race. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of race. No human review is needed as the classification is clear.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company," approved March 11, 1867.
classification.summary: This act revives the charter of the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company. It grants the company perpetual corporate rights and allows commissioners three years to open books for stock subscriptions. The company can organize and expend initial stock for surveys and further subscriptions once $10,000 in bona fide stock is subscribed. However, construction cannot begin until $1,000,000 (as per the original act) or $3,000,000 (as per this amendment) in bona fide subscriptions are obtained.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operational requirements of a railroad company. It details financial thresholds for organization and construction. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Notice of the election must be published in the Warsaw Weekly News and posted publicly. Violators are subject to fines. The act does not interfere with existing tavern licenses.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes for the benefit of common schools in the several counties of this Commonwealth," approved March 18, 1870.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for collecting taxes levied under a previous act for common schools. It specifies that sheriffs or designated city/town officers will collect these taxes, requiring them to execute bonds with sureties. The act outlines penalties for failure to collect and pay over taxes within the prescribed time, including liability for the unpaid amount plus ten percent, and forfeiture of office for failure to execute the required bond. It also assigns the duty to commissioners to oversee the collection process and initiate legal proceedings against delinquent officers.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville, and to Provide for the Collection of Taxes for the Sinking Fund
classification.summary: This act details the appointment and duties of commissioners for a sinking fund in counties, cities, or towns, including the appointment of a treasurer and the execution of bonds. It also outlines the collection of taxes levied under this act by sheriffs or other designated city/town officers, who must also execute bonds for faithful collection and payment. The act grants these officers the power to distrain and sell personal property for tax collection.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the administrative and financial mechanisms for collecting taxes and managing a sinking fund within counties, cities, and towns. It outlines the appointment of commissioners, the role of a treasurer, and the responsibilities of tax collectors (sheriffs and other officers). There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race within the text. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1871), indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

It outlines procedures for collecting taxes, receiving dividends, paying interest on bonds, and managing surplus funds through bond purchases or investments. It also addresses the process for issuing new bonds if existing funds are insufficient to meet bond obligations at maturity, including the possibility of levying a tax on taxable property.

classification.summary: This act details the process for issuing stock certificates to individuals who have paid taxes to defray interest on county, city, and town bonds used for the railroad. It outlines the duties of treasurers of sinking funds in issuing these certificates and receipts for fractional shares, and the railroad company's duty to issue actual stock upon presentation of these certificates. It also establishes lien rights for claimants and judgments against the company's property and mandates taxation of real estate to cover bond interest and principal.

The statute addresses general legal procedures for property acquisition that would apply to all citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Louisville, Memphis, and New Orleans Railway Company
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Louisville, Memphis, and New Orleans Railway Company. It authorizes county judges to submit to qualified voters the question of whether the county should subscribe to the capital stock of the railway company. If a majority votes in favor, the county judge is directed to make the subscription. The act also repeals certain previous sections and outlines the process for issuing bonds by counties, cities, or towns that subscribe to the stock.

It also specifies their powers regarding fines, imprisonment for contempt, issuing subpoenas, and collecting taxes and fines. The act also establishes the right of appeal for judgments rendered by the police judge.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Webster
classification.summary: This act grants powers to the trustees of the town of Webster, Kentucky, regarding street maintenance, public health nuisances, and infrastructure improvements. It details their authority to clear obstructions, erect cisterns and pumps, direct drainage, abate nuisances, regulate combustible materials, and ensure chimneys and fireplaces are safe. The trustees can also require property owners to pave sidewalks and streets, and if owners fail to comply, the trustees can execute the work at the owner's expense, with provisions for recovering costs through warrants or suits. The act also mentions concurrent jurisdiction with police judges and justices of the peace for certain matters.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and public works powers granted to the trustees of the town of Webster, Kentucky. It covers responsibilities such as street cleaning, drainage, and sidewalk construction. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted are general municipal governance functions. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act entitled "An act to reduce into one the several acts in relation to the government of the city of Henderson," approved March 10, 1856, and the several acts amendatory thereof.
classification.summary: This act makes several amendments to the charter of the city of Henderson. It modifies provisions related to the marshal's office, including surety requirements and vacancy filling. It also repeals a section that required inhabitants to pay county levies if they provided for the poor and maintained streets, and it repeals a property qualification for eligibility as a city councilman. Finally, it includes a separate act to incorporate the Public Library of Kentucky.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first act (sections 10-13) amends the charter of the city of Henderson, focusing on administrative and financial matters such as the marshal's office, tax levies, and councilman qualifications. The second act (Chapter 1947) incorporates the Public Library of Kentucky. Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as there is no Jim Crow classification. classification.summary: This act incorporates the Louisville Public Library, outlining its powers and responsibilities. It details how the corporation can acquire property, obtain books and other materials through various means including stock issuance, donations, and loans. The act emphasizes that the library is to be free for the gratuitous use and enjoyment of every citizen of Kentucky and other states, and must be kept open daily. It also authorizes the trustees to appoint agents and assistants to manage the library's operations and fundraising, with the caveat that the corporation should not incur debt beyond its means.

It specifies that trustees cannot hold city government positions, must execute bonds, and cannot be personally interested in school contracts. Trustees will not receive a salary. The city treasurer and clerk are required to keep separate accounts for school funds, and the board of trustees has exclusive control over these funds.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and financial governance of public schools in Lexington. It focuses on the qualifications, responsibilities, and financial oversight of school trustees and city officials related to school funds. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an amendment to a previous act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact an act entitled "An Act to establish a system of public schools in the city of Lexington," approved March 10, 1860, and the several acts amendatory thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends and re-enacts previous legislation establishing a public school system in Lexington, Kentucky. It details the powers and responsibilities of the board of school trustees, including the receipt and expenditure of funds, appointment of a president and secretary, reporting to the mayor and council, and exclusive control over teacher appointments, school discipline, curriculum, and examinations. It also grants the board the power to purchase, build, or rent grounds and buildings for public school purposes, with provisions for property reservation and exemption from city debt and taxation. The act also outlines reporting requirements for the trustees and allows for the sale, lease, or exchange of public school property, provided proceeds are reinvested in public school uses.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the organization and administration of public schools in Lexington, Kentucky. It outlines the powers of the school board, financial management, and property acquisition/disposal. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on the administrative and operational aspects of a public school system without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to authorize the county court of [County Name] to employ an attorney to defend suits instituted under the act of Congress approved May 31st, 1870, and to make allowances to public officers.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of a specific county (though the county name is omitted in the provided text) to employ an attorney to defend lawsuits filed against county officials under the Enforcement Act of 1870. It also allows the court to reimburse these officials for expenses incurred in defending these suits. The act specifically mentions the suit of D. G. Falconer against J. R. Mortor, the county attorney, and aims to test the constitutionality of the federal act.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses legal challenges arising from the Enforcement Act of 1870, which was enacted to enforce voting rights. While the Enforcement Act of 1870 was a federal law aimed at protecting the voting rights of newly freed slaves, this specific Kentucky statute is procedural. It concerns the authorization for a county to defend itself and its officers against lawsuits related to this federal act. The statute itself does not contain any discriminatory provisions based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines a legal defense strategy and does not exhibit any racial discrimination within its own provisions. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is null because the statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to reduce into one the several acts in regard to the Town of Somerset
classification.summary: This act consolidates previous legislation concerning the Town of Somerset. It defines the corporate limits of the town, establishes its jurisdiction and legal standing, and vests its fiscal, prudential, and municipal concerns in a Board of Trustees. The act also outlines the powers and responsibilities of this board.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to consolidate and define the corporate limits and governance of the Town of Somerset. It details geographical boundaries and the structure of the town's municipal government (Board of Trustees). There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 103 of the General Statutes, Title 'Towns,' So as to Apply to the Town of Somerset, Pulaski County.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the election process for trustees of the town of Somerset, Pulaski County. It specifies the qualifications for voters, including age, residency, and poll tax payment. It also details the duties of the trustees, the election procedures, and the oath of office.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the election of town trustees and the qualifications for voters. While it mentions poll taxes, which were sometimes used as a mechanism to disenfranchise Black voters during the Jim Crow era, there is no explicit or implicit racial component in the text provided. The qualifications are based on residency, age, and tax payment, which would apply to all eligible inhabitants regardless of race. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature. Confidence is high because the text is clear and does not suggest racial discrimination.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Somerset
classification.summary: This act details the rights of qualified voters to elect trustees, the procedures for trustee meetings and vacancies, and the powers of the board of trustees regarding town property, including real estate, streets, and alleys. It specifies how the board can acquire, manage, lease, or sell property, and outlines their authority to initiate legal actions to protect town assets. A key provision requires a recorded vote of four trustees for any sale or lease of real estate.

It covers aspects such as elections, meetings, property management, and legal authority. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1871.

classification.summary: This statute details the requirements for town officers in Somerset, Kentucky, including bond execution, attestation, and recording. It also outlines the appointment and duties of an annual assessor responsible for listing taxable property and individuals within the town. The assessor is to collect oaths from taxable persons and report on property owners, including those unknown, to the board of trustees.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and financial governance of the town of Somerset, specifically concerning the bonding of its officers and the process of property assessment for taxation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and financial, not related to racial segregation or disenfranchisement. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes therein.
classification.summary: This act outlines procedures for the assessment and collection of taxes in the city of Louisville. It details the roles of trustees, assessors, clerks, and treasurers in setting valuations, levying taxes, recording lists, and managing fiscal concerns. It also specifies procedures for aggrieved persons to appeal valuations and for the treasurer to handle funds and payments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and financial procedures for the city of Louisville, specifically concerning tax assessment and collection. It outlines the responsibilities of various officials and the processes for property valuation, tax levies, and financial management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the election and qualifications for the marshal and the bond requirements for these officials. The statute focuses on the administrative and financial operations of the town government.

It covers aspects like oath-taking, bond requirements, record-keeping, and tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 107 of the General Statutes, Title "Towns," So as to Provide for the Election of Trustees, and to Define Their Powers and Duties, and to Provide for the Election of a Marshal and Other Officers, and to Define Their Powers and Duties.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, powers, and fees of a town marshal and their deputies. It details their responsibilities in collecting taxes, serving processes, executing warrants, and taking bonds. The statute also grants the marshal powers similar to those of constables as peace officers and in civil actions. It specifies fees for various services, including serving peace warrants, warrants for by-laws, summoning witnesses, and serving felony warrants. The marshal is also authorized to take replevin bonds on executions.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative and enforcement roles of a town marshal and their deputies within a municipality. It details their responsibilities related to tax collection, serving legal documents, and executing warrants. The language used is procedural and administrative, focusing on the powers and compensation of these officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Somerset
classification.summary: This act details the process for the town of Somerset to collect unpaid taxes on lots. It outlines procedures for advertising delinquent properties, conducting sales to recover taxes, and the redemption period for property owners. It also addresses the appointment of town marshals.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for tax collection and property sales within the town of Somerset, Kentucky. It outlines the duties of the board of trustees and the marshal in advertising and selling delinquent properties to recover unpaid taxes. The text also includes provisions for filling vacancies in the office of marshal. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the legislature in a specific year (1871).

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Somerset
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Somerset the power to assess, levy, and collect various taxes, including property taxes, poll taxes on free male persons over 21, taxes on theatrical performances and exhibitions, and taxes on auctioneers. It also allows them to license auctioneers, drays, wagons, carts, hacks, and coaches. The trustees have the authority to prohibit the erection of wooden buildings or regulate their size and height, suppress tippling-houses, houses of ill-fame, disorderly houses, and regulate the retailing of spirituous liquors without a license. The act also outlines provisions for the redemption of grounds and penalties for defacing advertisements.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the powers of the town trustees of Somerset, Kentucky, regarding taxation, licensing, and regulation of various businesses and activities within the town. It also includes provisions for public order and safety. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Vanceburg
classification.summary: This act grants the trustees of Vanceburg the exclusive right to license and tax taverns, merchants, druggists, and coffee-house keepers within the town limits. It specifies the conditions for granting and revoking licenses for the sale of spirituous liquors and outlines procedures for street extensions and property acquisition. The statute also requires applicants for liquor licenses to meet state requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the local governance of a town, specifically the licensing and taxation of businesses, including those selling alcohol, and the regulation of public works like street extensions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The powers granted to the trustees are regulatory in nature and apply to all individuals and businesses within the town's jurisdiction without racial distinction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute details the jurisdiction and powers of the police judge of the town of Somerset. It outlines their authority over misdemeanors, civil and criminal cases within the town limits, including the power to require bail, issue summons, and fine or imprison for contempt. It also specifies jurisdiction over offenses arising from town by-laws and ordinances.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the judicial powers of a police judge in the town of Somerset. It enumerates the types of cases the judge can hear and the powers they possess, such as issuing warrants, taking bail, and handling contempt of court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be a standard grant of judicial authority to a local official. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act, entitled "An act to provide for the assessment of property for taxation," approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning property assessment for taxation. It specifies that the county assessor shall, in addition to other duties, assess all property within their county, including "all lands, town lots, and other real estate, and all slaves, horses, mules, jacks, jennets, cattle, sheep, hogs, and all other stock, and all furniture, watches, jewelry, and all other personal property, and all money, notes, bonds, stocks, and all other choses in action, and all other property, real and personal, that may be owned or in the possession of any person, firm or corporation, on the first day of January in each year."
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'slaves' in the context of property to be assessed for taxation. While slavery was abolished by the 13th Amendment in 1865, its inclusion in a 1871 statute indicates a lingering legal framework or a transitional period where such terminology might still appear in legislation, even if the practice was no longer legal. However, the primary focus of the statute is property taxation and assessment, not the regulation of racial groups in the manner of typical Jim Crow laws (e.g., segregation, disenfranchisement, anti-miscegenation). The mention of 'slaves' is a historical artifact within the context of property law rather than an active discriminatory provision. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law, but the explicit racial indicator is noted due to the term 'slaves'.
